content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 16, 2010

Brian T. Hildreth
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-031

Dear Mr. Hildreth:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it tenders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice
is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if
any, of other conflict of interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government
Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty participate in a governmental
decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the sole
shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property that is located
within the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan?

CONCLUSION

Yes, Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty may participate in the
governmental decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the
sole shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property located in
the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan if there will be no reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon his economic interests.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are County Counsel for Riverside County and are seeking advice on behalf of
Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty, who represents the County's third district.
The County of Riverside has a general plan that is composed of many different community plans,
each of which includes thousands of individual parcels. Riverside County is in the process of
revising one of its community plans (the "Southwest Area Plan") and creating a new community
plan within the current Southwest Area Plan. The community plan would be called the
"Temecula Valley Wine County Community Plan" (TVWCCP). This new plan will encompass
in excess of 19,200 acres and 2,400 separate ownership parcels.

The Riverside County Planning Commission does not have approval authority over the
proposed new plan, however, the Planning Commission will hold public hearings and make
recommendations to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors as to the plan's boundaries,
design guidelines and permitted uses within the plan. The Planning Commission will
subsequently take a vote to recommend to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors the
boundaries, design guidelines and permitted uses within the TVWCCP. The Planning
Commission will not make a final decision on any aspect of the proposed new community plan.
That decision I made by the Board of Supervisors. The Planning Commission is strictly a
recommending body.

Additionally, the inclusion of any parcel in the TVWCCP will confer no vested right for
any development, it will only allow for greater flexibility as to what a property owner may
propose in the future. Any potential development plan for a specific property would still have to
come before the Planning Commission as a separate application. These individual applications
would require their own separate environmental analysis and other review and would be subject
to the discretionary approval of the Planning Commission followed by an additional separate
ratification action by the Board of Supervisors.

Part of Commissioner Petty's professional career outside of the Planning Commission is
working as a consultant to a corporation whose sole shareholder owns property, in his individual
name, within the proposed limits of the TVWCCP. Commissioner Petty has received income of
$500 or more from the corporation in the past and will likely receive income from the
corporation in the future. The corporation has no real property interest within the TVWCCP or
the County of Riverside, California. Its principal business is medical management and medically
related ventures. Commissioner Petty consults specifically on those types of matters for the
corporation. He does not provide any consultation services to the individual shareholder. The
corporation has no real property interest within Riverside County.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 3

analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of his or her financial interests.

Step 1. Is Commissioner Petty a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency." As a member of the Riverside County Planning
Commission, which is a local government agency, Commissioner Petty is a public official.
Therefore, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to
influence any governmental decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of his economic interests.

Step 2. Will Commissioner Petty be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that is before
his or her agency when, for the purposes of influencing the decision, the official contacts or
appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant
of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) A public official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision that is before an agency, other than the
official's agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to
act, on behalf of, or as the representative of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).)

The Riverside Planning Commission will hold public hearings on the proposed
TVWCCP and the members of the Planning Commission will subsequently take a vote or votes
to recommend to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors the boundaries, design guidelines
and permitted uses within the TVWCCP. Because such votes would commit his agency to a
course of action, should Planning Commissioner Petty participate, he would be making
governmental decisions.

Step 3. What are Commissioner Petty's economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 87103
and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Because Commissioner Petty works as a consultant for the corporation you have described and has received income of $500 or more within the past 12 months, the corporation is a source of income to him. Therefore, Commissioner Petty has an economic interest in the corporation.

For conflict-of-interest purposes, the Commission has "pierced" through entities, such as corporations, to consider the nature of the relationship between the entity and the person or persons who control the entity. When this piercing occurs, the economic interest is deemed to exist in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6.; Lahr Advice Letter, No. I-98-298 (public official who has an interest in a corporation also has an economic interest in the corporation's sole shareholder).)

The Commission has advised that in the corporate context, "the controlling shareholder in a close corporation is one who holds a majority of the stock. Because corporate control is based ultimately on stock voting rights, a shareholder who holds the majority of the votes effectively holds control." (Nord, supra.) A majority shareholder is deemed to have control over the business entity such that the business entity and the shareholder would be considered one and the same for purposes of identifying economic interests. (In re Nord, supra; Lahr, supra; Hahn Advice Letter, No. I-91-311.) Therefore, because the corporation is a source of income, and the

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 5

sole shareholder effectively holds control of the corporation, Commissioner Petty has an
economic interest in the corporation as well as in the shareholder as a source of income.

Because you have not indicated any other possible economic interests, only
Commissioner Petty's source of income economic interests in the corporation and the sole
shareholder of the corporation will be further analyzed.

Step 4. Determine whether Commissioner Petty's economic interests are directly or
indirectly involved in a governmental decision.

Regulation 18704.1 provides the rules for determining whether or not a source of income
is directly or indirectly involved in a decision:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts,
is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:

"(1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Because neither the corporation nor the sole shareholder of the corporation has initiated
the proceedings and are not named parties or subject of the proceedings, Commissioner Petty's
economic interests are not directly involved in the governmental decision. If a public official's
economic interests are not directly involved in a governmental decision they are indirectly
involved. (Regulation 18704(a).)

Steps 5 and 6. Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
Commissioner Petty's economic interest?

Regulation 18705.1(c) (copy enclosed) describes the standards used for determining
when there is a material financial effect on a business entity that is a source of income to a public
official if the business entity is indirectly involved in the governmental decision. You have not
provided us with the information necessary to determine which materiality standard would apply
to the corporation; therefore, determining the applicable materiality standard will be up to
Commissioner Petty.2

2 Information needed in order to determine which materiality standard to apply to the corporation would
include the corporation's revenues, net income or if it is listed on the New York Stock exchange, NASDAQ, or the
American Stock exchange. (See Regulation 18705.1(c).)

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 6


Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) describes the standards for determining if there is a material
financial effect on an individual who is a source of income to a public official, and who is
indirectly involved in a governmental decision.

"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a source of
income to an official if any of the following applies:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by
$1,000 or more; or

"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, California Code of Regulations,
sections 18705.2(b)."

"Real property that is indirectly involved in a governmental decision is
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that
there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial
effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but
are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

(Regulation 18705.2(b))

Because you have not provided information on how the decision might affect the
shareholder's income, investments, or other tangible assets or liabilities we cannot
determine if there will be a material financial effect on Commissioner Petty's economic
interest, that determination will be up to Commissioner Petty.

In addition, Commissioner Petty will also need to determine if the decision will
affect the sole shareholder's real property interest in a manner that is considered material.

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 7

If it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the above materiality standards will be met as a
result of the governmental decision then Commissioner Petty will have a conflict of interest. A
financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely that
one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that economic interest will be met as a
result of the governmental decision.

Segmentation

We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has a disqualifying
interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other components in which
the official has no financial interest. (Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542.) This process is
called "segmentation."

Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:

"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;

"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and

"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the
result of another decision."

Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions
that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which Commissioner Petty has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. The Commission has advised that if the resolution of one
decision will in any way affect the decision from which a public official is disqualified,

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 8

the decisions may not be segmented. (Barker Advice Letter, No. A-03-022; McLaughlin Advice
Letter, No. A-02-132; Ennis Advice Letter.)

Though it is the Board of Supervisors who will make the final decision as to what
provisions will go into the TVWCCP, the recommendations that the Planning Commission will
make will likely have significant influence and effect on the decisions of the County Board of
Supervisors. Therefore, they will effectively determine, affirm, modify, or alter the result of the
County Board of Supervisors decisions. Because of this, the decisions that the Planning
Commission will make are "inextricably interrelated" to those of the County Board of
Supervisors and cannot be segmented.

Additionally, the segmentation analysis will not change simply because a property owner
would have to make a separate application to the Planning Commission or Board of Supervisors
to take advantage of any new land uses provided for in the TVWCCP, because the possibility of
those new land uses depends upon the decisions at hand.

You have also asked whether removing the shareholder's property from the TVWCCP
would eliminate the possibility of a conflict of interest for Commissioner Petty. Simply
excluding only the shareholder's property from the decision would not remove the conflict for Commissioner Petty in order
to allow him to participate in the decision without a conflict of interest. You have stated that the
TVWCCP would grant the same entitlements to all property owners included in the plan,
therefore it would seem likely that the sole shareholder's property, even if removed from the
decision, would be affected by the decision if the rest of the properties that were included in the
TVWCCP were near or surrounded the shareholder's property.

Step 7 and 8. Do the Public Generally or the Legally Required Participation exceptions
Apply to Commissioner Petty?

Under the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a decision if
the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) This "public
generally" exception is codified in Regulations 18707 - 18707.9. Pursuant to these provisions,
two prongs must be satisfied in order for the public generally exception to apply: 1. A significant
segment of the public generally must be affected by the governmental decision and 2. The
significant segment of the public generally must be affected in substantially the same manner.
(Regulation 18707.1.)

A significant segment of the public generally for economic interests who are sources of
income is defined as:

"(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency or the district the official represents; or

File No. A-10-031
Page No. 9

"(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction."

(Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(A).)

You have stated that the decisions will convey the same possible rights upon every land owner whose real property is included in the TVWCCP. However, you have not provided any facts indicating that a significant segment of the jurisdiction or district that Commissioner Petty Represents would be affected by this decision in the same way that Commission Petty's economic interest would be affected. You will need to determine if at least 10 percent of the population in the jurisdiction or district that Commissioner Petty Represents or if at least 5,000 individuals in the jurisdiction will be a part of the TVWCCP to satisfy the first prong of the public generally exception.

If you determine that Commissioner Petty has satisfied the first prong of the public generally exception you will next need to determine whether each person identified as part of the significant segment will be affected in substantially the same manner. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(2).) Criteria that can be used to determine whether the significant segment will be affected in substantially the same manner can be found in Regulation 18707.1(b)(2)(A) (copy enclosed). Though Regulation 18707.1(b)(2)(A) describes criteria for real property owned by a public official, the regulation provides examples of how to determine when a real property owner will be affected in substantially the same manner as others and will be helpful in making this determination for Commissioner Petty when looking to see if a significant segment of the public will be affected in substantially the same manner as Commissioner Petty's economic interest would be.

There are also circumstances where a public official may be required to take part in a governmental decision despite a disqualifying conflict of interest, when necessary to provide a quorum that allows the agency to take action. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.) This is called the "legally required participation" exception. You have not presented any facts that indicate that the legally required participation exception is implicated in Commissioner Petty's current situation, thus it will not be analyzed here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
Enclosures

sections.question:
May Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty participate in a govemmental
decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the sole
shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property that is located
within the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty may participate in the
governmental decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the
sole shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property located in
the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan if there will be no reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon his economic interests.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 9 I 0 14. , unless otherwise indicated.

sections.facts:
You are County Counsel for Riverside County and are seeking advice on behalf of
Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty, who represents the County's third district.
The County of Riverside has a general plan that is composed of many different community plans,
each of which includes thousands of individual parcels. Riverside County is in the process of
revising one of its community plans (the "Southwest Area Plan") and creating a new community
plan within the current Southwest Area Plan. The community plan would be called the
"Temecula Valley Wine County Community Plan" (TVWCCP). This new plan will encompass
in excess of 19,200 acres and 2,400 separate ownership parcels.
The Riverside County Planning Commission does not have approval authority over the
proposed new plan, however, the Planning Commission will hold public hearings and make
recorlmendations to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors as to the plan's boundaries,
design guidelines and permitted uses within the plan. The Planning Commission will
subsequently take a vote to recommend to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors the
boundaries, design guidelines and permitted uses within the TVWCCP. The Planning
Commission will not make a final decision on any aspect of the proposed new cofilmunity plan.
That decision I made by the Board of Supervisors. The Planning Commission is strictly a
recommending body.
Additionally, the inclusion of any parcel in the TVWCCP will confer no vested right for
any development, it will only allow for greater flexibility as to what a property owner may
propose in the future. Any potential development plan for a specific property would still have to
come before the Planning Commission as a separate application. These individual applications
would require their own separate environmental analysis and other review and would be subject
to the discretionary approval of the Planning Commission followed by an additional separate
ratification action by the Board of Supervisors.
Part of Commissioner Petty's professional career outside of the Planning Commission is
working as a consultant to a corporation whose sole shareholder owns property, in his individual
name, within the proposed limits of the TVWCCP. Commissioner Petty has received income of
$500 or more from the corporation in the past and will likely receive income from the
corporation in the future. The corporation has no real property interest within the TVWCCP or
the County of Riverside, California. Its principal business is medical management and medically
related ventures. Commissioner Petty consults specifically on those types of matters for the
corporation. He does not provide any consultation services to the individual shareholder. The
corporation has no real property interest within Riverside County.
AIIALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
sections.analysis:
for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on one or more of his or her financial interests.
Step 1. Is Commissioner Petty a ('public official" within the meaning of Section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local govemment agency." As a member of the Riverside County Planning
Commission, which is a local government agency, Commissioner Petty is a public official.
Therefore, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to
influence any governmental decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of his economic interests.
Step 2. Will Commissioner Petty be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation t8702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision
makerregarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that is before
his or her agency when, for the purposes of influencing the decision, the official contacts or
appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, ffiy member, officer, employee, or consultant
of his or her agency. (Regulation I8702.3(a).) A public official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision that is before an agency, other than the
fficial's agency if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to
act, on behalf of, or as the representative of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).)
The Riverside Planning Commission will hold public hearings on the proposed
TVWCCP and the members of the Planning Commission will subsequently take a vote or votes
to recommend to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors the boundaries, design guidelines
and permitted uses within the TVWCCP. Because such votes would commit his agency to a
course of action, should Planning Commissioner Petty participate, he would be making
governmental decisions.
Step 3. What are Commissioner Petty's economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section 87103
and Regulations 18703- I 8703.5, inclusive:


a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703. l(a).)
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
r8703.2.)
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 187 03.4.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
a
a
a
a
Because Commissioner Petty works as a consultant for the corporation you have
described and has received income of $500 or more within the past 12 months, the corporation is
a source of income to him. Therefore, Commissioner Petty has an economic interest in the
corporation.
For conflict-of-interest purposes, the Commission has "pierced" through entities, such as
corporations, to consider the nature of the relationship between the entity and the person or
persons who control the entity. When this piercing occurs, the economic interest is deemed to
exist in the owning or controlling person(s) as well as the business entity. (In re Nord (1983) I
FPPC Ops. 6.); lnhr Advice Letter, No. I-98-298 (public official who has an economic interest
in a corporation also has an economic interest in the corporation's sole shareholder).)
The Commission has advised that in the corporate context, "the controlling shareholder in
a close corporation is one who holds a majority of the stock. Because corporate control is based
ultimately on stock voting rights, a shareholder who holds the majority of the votes effectively
holds control." (Nord, supra.) A majority shareholder is deemed to have control over the
business entity such that the business entity and the shareholder would be considered one and the
same for purposes of identifying economic interests. (ln re Nord, supra; Lahr, supra; Hahn
Advice Letter, No. I-91-3 1 1.) Therefore, because the corporation is a source of income, and the
sole shareholder effectively holds control of the corporation, Commissioner Petty has an
economic interest in the corporation as well as in the shareholder as a source of income.
Because you have not indicated any other possible economic interests, only
Commissioner Petty's source of income economic interests in the corporation and the sole
shareholder of the corporation will be further analyzed.
Step 4. Determine whether Commissioner Petty's economic interests are directly or
indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
Regulation 18704Jprovides the rules for determining whether or not a source of income
is directly or indirectly involved in a decision:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts,
is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
"(l) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Because neither the corporation nor the sole shareholder of the corporation has initiated
the prqceedings and are not named parties or subject of the proceedings, Commissioner Petty's
economic interests are not directly involved in the govemmental decision. If a public official's
economic interests are not directly involved in a govemmental decision they are indirectly
involved. (Regulation 1870a(a).)
Steps 5 and 6. Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
Commissioner Petty's economic interest?
Regulation 18705.1(c) (copy enclosed) describes the standards used for determining
when there is a material financial effect on a business entity that is a source of income to a public
official if the business entity is indirectly involved in the governmental decision. you have not
provided us with the information necessary to determine which materiality standard would apply
to the corporation; therefore, determining the applicable materiality standard will be up to
Commissioner Petty.2
2 Information needed in order to determine which materiality standard to apply to the corporation would
include the corporation's revenues, net income or if it is listed on the New York Stock exchange, NASDAe, or the
American Stock exchange. (See Regulation 18705.I (c).)
Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) describes the standards for determining if there is a material
financial effect on an individual who is a source of income to a public oificial, and who is
indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
"The effect of a decision is material as to an individual who is a source of
income to an official if any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by
$1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, Califomia Code of Regulations,
sections I8705.2(b)."
"Real property that is indirectly involved in a governmental decision is
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that
there are specific circumstances regarding the govemmental decision, its financial
effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an
economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has an
interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but
are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not rimited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
(Regulation I 8705.2(b))
Because you have not provided information on how the decision might affect the
shareholder's income, investments, or other tangible assets or liabilities we ianrrot
determine if there will be a material financial effect on Commissioner Petty's economic
interest, that determination will be up to Commissioner petty.
In addition, Commissioner Petty will also need to determine if the decision will
affect the sole shareholder's real property interest in a manner that is considered material.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the above materiality standards will be met as a
result of the govemmental decision then Commissioner Petty will have a conflict of interest. A
financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely that
one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that economic interest will be met ai a
result of the governmental decision.
Segmentation
We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who las a disqualifying
interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other components in which
the official has no financial interest. (Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542.f fnis process is
called "segmentation."
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial inrerest;
"(2)The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official *ur dirquulified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, ,ullify, or alter the
result of another decision."
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions
that are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which Commissioner petty has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. The Commission has advised that if the resoiution of one
decision will in atry way affect the decision from which a public official is disqualified,
the decisions may not be segmented. (Barker Advice Letter, No. 4-03-022; McLaughlin Advice
Letter, No. A-02-132; Ennis Advice Letter.)
Though it is the Board of Supervisors who will make the final decision as to what
prwisions will go into the TvwCCP, the recommendations that the planning Commission will
make will likely have significant influence and effect on the decisions of thetounty Board of
Supervisors. Therefore, they will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the
County Board of Supervisors decisions. Because of this, the decisioni that the planning
Commission will make are "inextricably interrelated" to those of the County Board of
Supervisors and cannot be segmented.
Additionally, the segmentation analysis will not change simply because a property owner
would have to make a separate application to lhe Planning commisiion or Board Lr Sup"*iro6
to take advantage of any new land uses provided for in the TVWCCP, because the possibility of
those new land uses depends upon the decisions at hand.
You have also asked whether removing the shareholder's property from the TVWCCp
would eliminate the possibility of a conflict of interest for Commissioneiletty. Simply
excluding only the shareholder's property from the decision would not equate to Urirrg u different
component of the plan and would likely not remove the conflict for Commissioner reity in order
to allow him to participate in the decision without a conflict of interest. you have stated that the
TVWCCP would grant the same entitlements to all property owners included in the plan,
therefore it would seem likely that the sole shareholteris pioperty, even if removed from the
decision, would be affected by the decision if the rest of tire proplrties that were included in the
TVwccP were near or surrounded the shareholder's property.
Step 7 and 8. Do the Public Generally or the Legatly Required Participation exceptions
Apply to Commissioner petty?
Under the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a decision if
the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interesi is indistinguishable from
the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation l8707ia).) This ..public
generally" exception is.codified in Regulations 18707 - 18707.9. pursuant to these provisions,
two prongs must be satisfied in order for th-e public generally exception to apply: l. A significant
segment of the public generally must be affected by the goverrmental decision and 2. The
significant segment of the public generally must be affecJed in substantially the same manner.
(Regulation 18707.1.)
A significant segment of the public generally for economic interests who are sources of
income is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency or the district the official represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction."
(Regulation I 8707. l(b)(l )(A).)
You have stated that the decisions will convey the same possible rights upon every land
owner whose real property is included in the TVwCaP. Howeier, you haie noiprovidea *y
facts indicating that a significant segment of the jurisdiction or district that Commissioner petty
represents would be affected by this decision in the same way that Commissioner petty's
economic interest would be affected. You will need to determine if at least l0 percent of the
population in the jurisdiction or district that Commissioner Petty Represents or if at least 5,000
individuals in the jurisdiction will be a part of the TvwCCp to iatisfy the first prong of the
public generally exception.
If you determine that Commissioner Petty has satisfied the first prong of the public
generally exception you will next need to determine whether each person idJntified as part of the
significant segment will be affected in substantially the same manner. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(2).) Criteria that can be used to determine whether the significint segment will be
affected in substantially the same manner can be found in Regulatio, t8ZOZ.t(b)(2)(A) (copy
enclosed)' Though Regulation 18707.1(b)(2XA) describes criteria for real property owned by a
publtc official, the regulation provides examples of how to determine when a real iroperty o*r",
will be affected in substantially the same manner as others and will be helpful in making this.
determination for Commissioner Petty when looking to see if a significunir"g-.nt of the public
will be affected in substantially the same manner as Commissione-r petty's eclonomic interest
would be.
There are also circumstances where a public official may be required to take part in a
governmental decision despite a disqualifying conflict of interest, when necessary to provide a
quorum that allows the agency to take action. (Section 87101; Regulation lg70g.) This is called
the "legally required participation" exception. You have not presented any facts that indicate
that the legally required participation ex-eption is implicatedln Commissioner petty,s current
situation, thus it will not be analyzed here.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty participate in a govemmental
decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the sole
shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property that is located
within the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty may participate in the
governmental decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the
sole shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property located in
the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan if there will be no reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon his economic interests.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 9 I 0 14. , unless otherwise indicated.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 16, 2010 Brian T. Hildreth Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP 455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-031 Dear Mr. Hildreth: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it tenders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict of interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION May Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty participate in a governmental decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the sole shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property that is located within the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan? CONCLUSION Yes, Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty may participate in the governmental decision involving the Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan when the sole shareholder of a corporation from which he receives income owns real property located in the proposed Temecula Valley Wine Country Community Plan if there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon his economic interests. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-031 Page No. 2 FACTS You are County Counsel for Riverside County and are seeking advice on behalf of Riverside County Planning Commissioner John Petty, who represents the County's third district. The County of Riverside has a general plan that is composed of many different community plans, each of which includes thousands of individual parcels. Riverside County is in the process of revising one of its community plans (the "Southwest Area Plan") and creating a new community plan within the current Southwest Area Plan. The community plan would be called the "Temecula Valley Wine County Community Plan" (TVWCCP). This new plan will encompass in excess of 19,200 acres and 2,400 separate ownership parcels. The Riverside County Planning Commission does not have approval authority over the proposed new plan, however, the Planning Commission will hold public hearings and make recommendations to the Riverside County Board of Supervisors as to the plan's boundaries, design guidelines and permitted uses within the plan. The Planning Commission will subsequently take a vote to recommend to the Riverside County Board
